game_name: Age of Empires II: The Age of Kings

element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Players learn resource gathering mechanics with peaceful civilian units before military engagement

element_introduction_order.element: Four resource types (food, wood, gold, stone)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Resource management is foundational; depository buildings teach checkpoint-based logistics

element_introduction_order.element: Basic buildings (town center, mill, lumber yard, mining camp)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Spatial resource infrastructure teaches base positioning and supply chains
Limited unit roster prevents overwhelming new players; teaches fundamentals
Requires building prerequisite structures and paying resource costs; gates content progression
New military units unlock; players begin armed conflict and defense
Combat introduced after resource and building mastery; allows peaceful early expansion
Advanced military units and defensive structures; mid-game power spike

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Available from early game, heavily used in mid/late game
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Alternative economy path; fluctuating prices and long-distance trading create risk/reward
Passive income system; units can be intercepted by enemies, adding vulnerability
Territorial control mechanic; religious buildings compete with military focus
Final age unlocks highest-tier units and upgrades; end-game tech rush

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Campaign mode (5 scenarios: Genghis Khan, Barbarossa, Saladin, Joan of Arc, William Wallace)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Story-driven conditions modify standard RTS rules; specialized objectives teach strategic variation

typical_relief_triggers: Advancing to a new Age (unlocks new units/structures, temporary resource investment breathing room)
Trade route completion generating passive income
Defeating enemy threats or achieving campaign objectives
typical_tension_triggers: Prerequisite building requirements for Age advancement blocking progression
Trade unit vulnerability during long-distance trips (enemy interception risk)
Military unit counter-strategies (hard counters forcing unit composition adjustment)
Campaign-specific story constraints limiting strategic options
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Four-Age progression creates macro rhythm; early game is calm and exploratory, mid-game escalates military pressure, late-game becomes intense with full unit rosters and competing civilizations at peak power. Cyclical resource gathering and unit training creates micro-rhythm within macro ages.

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Relic locations in monasteries are optional objectives; players can ignore religious victory conditions and focus on military domination
spatial_grammar.secret_convention: Gold deposits and relics reward map exploration; distant trade routes yield higher rewards but greater vulnerability

difficulty_curve.early_game: Very gentle. Dark Age provides only basic units and structures; players focus on peaceful resource gathering and base building. Minimal military threat allows learning curve.
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Moderate escalation. Feudal and Castle Ages unlock diverse military units; AI opponents become aggressive. Players must balance economy with defense and begin offensive operations.
difficulty_curve.late_game: High intensity. Imperial Age unlocks final unit tiers and technology upgrades. Multiple civilizations reach peak power simultaneously; games become battles of attrition, unit composition, and macro-management.
notable_spikes: First transition to Feudal Age (requires prerequisite building + resource investment; gates new units)
Castle Age unlock (introduces siege weapons and advanced military structures)
Imperial Age unlock (highest resource cost; typically decisive for victory)
Campaign-specific difficulty spikes (Joan of Arc, William Wallace, Genghis Khan scenarios impose unique constraints)

notable_level_patterns.description: Four sequential ages (Dark → Feudal → Castle → Imperial) unlock new units, buildings, and technologies. Each age requires constructing specific buildings and paying cumulative resource costs, creating natural pacing checkpoints and power-scaling gates.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Any campaign scenario; all skirmish and multiplayer games

notable_level_patterns.description: Four distinct resource types (food, wood, gold, stone) are harvested from different map locations (hunting grounds, forests, mines, fishing). Players must establish and defend multiple territorial zones, creating spatial resource distribution puzzle.
13 civilizations possess unique units, technologies, and bonuses (e.g., one civ may excel at cavalry, another at archers). Encourages varied playstyles and counters across matches.

5 historical campaigns (Genghis Khan, Barbarossa, Saladin, Joan of Arc, William Wallace) impose narrative-driven conditions and objectives that deviate from standard skirmish rules, teaching strategic problem-solving under constraints.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Campaign mode (Genghis Khan invasion of Eurasia, Barbarossa's Crusade, Saladin's defense of Holy Land)

notable_level_patterns.description: Market and dock trading systems allow generating gold without mining, creating an alternative economy path. Trade routes reward longer distances with higher income but expose units to enemy interception, creating risk/reward tension.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: All game modes (particularly relevant in multiplayer with resource competition)
